role_summary: Participant in the 1960 Richmond, Virginia lunch-counter sit-in movement during her undergraduate years at Virginia Union University; later teacher in the Los Angeles public schools; interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project in Ojai, California in 2013.
ai_reading: Grinnell's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 72), with Virginia Simms George (entry 122) as the top match and the broader cluster including Harold K. Brown (entry 55, San Diego CORE), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, SNCC Northern support), and Mildred Pitts Walter (entry 91). The novel observation her page surfaces is the California-teacher-to-1960-sit-in-veteran signature: Grinnell's California-grown childhood, her Virginia Union College years that overlapped with the 1960 sit-in movement, and her return to Los Angeles for a teaching career, follow a less-photographed civil-rights-organizer-into-public-education arc the catalog tracks in only a few other figures. The Virginia Simms George cosine is the catalog's clearest example of a California-rooted-civil-rights-veteran pairing.
biographical_paragraph: While Grinnell's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 48 [src: 1]. Gloria Claudette Collins Grinnell grew up in California. She attended Virginia Union University in Richmond, Virginia, where she participated in the 1960 lunch-counter sit-in movement that swept Southern colleges in the months following the February 1960 Greensboro Woolworth's sit-in. After returning to California she worked as a teacher in the Los Angeles public school system. Her oral history was conducted by David P. Cline in Ojai, California in April 2013 [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
interview_snippets.lead_in: Grinnell describes the sit-in at the Thalhimer's department store lunch counter in downtown Richmond, where her height made her conspicuous to the staff:
interview_snippets.quote: We went downtown several days and we went to Thalhimer's department store and we sat down. Well, I didn't sit down. I was standing, but being so tall, they pointed me out really intruly. Because I stand out and I had on high heels shoes too. So that made me over six feet tall. And they just wouldn't serve us.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked what she carried back to California from the sit-in years, Grinnell distinguishes legal change from a change in people:
interview_snippets.quote: But you can change laws, but you can't change people. It comes about through your doing individually.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
interview_snippets.lead_in: Grinnell recounts how the arrest record from her sit-in participation followed her into later job applications, including one in Washington, D.C.:
interview_snippets.quote: It did hurt me job-wise. I went for a job in D.C., And the guy said, I have to hire you. But because you made a high score in the exam. But I don't think you would be happy here. He says, I see you've been involved in civil rights. And because I always put down that I have an arrest record.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
interview_snippets.lead_in: Grinnell describes a conversation with a young teacher at a school library, which she says was the moment she understood the historical weight of what she had taken part in:
interview_snippets.quote: In fact, I didn't realize it until I was at a school. And I was in the library. And the lady says, Dr. Grinnell, do you know anything about the Civil Rights Movement? And I said, yeah, I do. I said, I was in it. She says, you were? Oh, no, not a big deal. You know, then I realized she's young. She's a young teacher. And that's when it hit me. I guess it was a big deal.

movement_context: The 1960 student lunch-counter sit-in wave Grinnell joined at Virginia Union University traces its proximate trigger to February 1, 1960, when four North Carolina Agricultural and Technical College freshmen (Ezell A. Blair Jr., Franklin McCain, Joseph McNeil, and David Richmond) sat at the segregated Woolworth's lunch counter in Greensboro, North Carolina, and remained when service was refused [src: 2]. The action propagated rapidly through historically Black college and university (HBCU) campuses across the upper South during February and March 1960, reaching Nashville (Fisk University, American Baptist Theological Seminary, Tennessee A&I), Atlanta (the Atlanta University Center consortium), and Richmond, where the Virginia Union University student body launched its own sit-in campaign on February 20, 1960 [src: 3]. The Richmond campaign targeted the segregated lunch counters at the downtown Thalhimers and Miller and Rhoads department stores, the Woolworth's and Murphy's chain locations, and the Richmond Room at Thalhimers; the February 22, 1960 mass arrest of thirty-four Virginia Union students at Thalhimers (the group later memorialized as the Richmond 34) was the campaign's defining direct-action event and the case that produced the 1963 United States Supreme Court ruling in Randolph v. Virginia, which vacated the trespass convictions of the Richmond 34 alongside parallel sit-in convictions from other Southern cities [src: 3, 4]. Virginia Union University, founded in 1865 as one of the principal Reconstruction-era institutions of Black higher education in the upper South, had been a planning hub for the Richmond campaign through the leadership of student-body president Charles Sherrod (later a SNCC field secretary in Albany, Georgia) and the participation of faculty including Wyatt Tee Walker, who served as pastor of Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg before joining the Southern Christian Leadership Conference as Martin Luther King Jr.'s executive director in 1960 [src: 3, 5]. The April 1960 organizing conference at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina that Ella Baker convened with SCLC funding produced the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) out of the same student-sit-in cohort the Virginia Union campaign belonged to; SNCC's founding membership included delegations from Virginia Union and the other Richmond-area HBCUs alongside the Greensboro, Nashville, and Atlanta student leaderships [src: 4, 5]. The California-to-Virginia-Union educational pathway Grinnell's biography traces was a regular pattern among California-raised African American students of her generation, as the segregation of Southern higher education combined with the strong HBCU recruitment network drew students from Black communities across the country into the upper South for college; the return migration to West Coast public-school teaching careers after graduation similarly traces a recognizable cohort pattern documented in the broader social history of the African American Great Migration's second generation [src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: Independent scholarly or institutional reception of Gloria Claudette Grinnell specifically has not been established in the standard secondary literature on the 1960 Richmond sit-in movement as of this iteration of the catalog. The Library of Congress oral history is the principal extant first-person record available [src: 1]. The institutional memorialization of the Richmond 34 sit-in campaign through the Virginia Union University Richmond 34 Plaza dedication, the Virginia state historical-marker program, and the scholarly accounts of the Richmond civil rights movement focuses principally on the named arrestees of the February 22, 1960 Thalhimers action and the named campaign leadership (Charles Sherrod, Frank Pinkston, Elizabeth Johnson Rice, Carolyn Horne Quarles); the broader student-body participation in the wider sit-in campaign that ran from February through the summer of 1960, which Grinnell's biographical framing places her within, is documented at the cohort level rather than at the named-individual level in the existing institutional record [src: 3, 4]. The Los Angeles Unified School District teacher cohort that included Grinnell's later career has not been systematically documented as a civil-rights-veteran cohort in published scholarship; the conservative reading of the available record is that her oral history is the documentary anchor for both the sit-in-participation and California-teaching portions of her biography. Further institutional reception work would require archival research at the Virginia Union University L. Douglas Wilder Library and Learning Resource Center (which holds the Sherrod papers and related Richmond movement materials) and at the Special Collections of the California State Archives or the UCLA Library Special Collections (which hold materials documenting the Los Angeles civil rights veterans network).

gallery.alt: Pickford Hall on the Virginia Union University campus, Richmond, Virginia
gallery.caption: Pickford Hall on the Virginia Union University campus in Richmond, Virginia. Virginia Union, founded in 1865 as one of the principal Reconstruction-era institutions of Black higher education in the upper South, was the academic home Grinnell attended during her undergraduate years, and the institutional base of the February 1960 Richmond student sit-in campaign that produced the Richmond 34 Thalhimers arrests of February 22, 1960. The campus is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

gallery.alt: Greensboro Woolworth's lunch counter segment displayed at the National Museum of American History, Washington DC
gallery.caption: A segment of the F.W. Woolworth's lunch counter from Greensboro, North Carolina, now held at the Smithsonian National Museum of American History. The February 1, 1960 sit-in by four North Carolina Agricultural and Technical College freshmen at this counter was the proximate trigger for the student sit-in wave that reached Virginia Union University in February 1960 and that Grinnell's biographical framing places her within. The Richmond, Virginia campaign launched on February 20, 1960 and produced the Richmond 34 mass arrest at Thalhimers two days later.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (artifact held by the National Museum of American History)

gallery.alt: 1960 Lunch Counter Sit-Ins United States Postal Service 37-cent commemorative stamp, 2005
gallery.caption: The 37-cent United States Postal Service commemorative stamp issued in 2005 as part of the To Form a More Perfect Union Black History Month panel marking the 1960 lunch counter sit-in movement. The stamp's image is drawn from a National Civil Rights Museum exhibit and was designed by Ethel Kessler with the museum exhibit sculpture by StudioEIS. The stamp commemorates the broader 1960 student sit-in wave that included the Richmond, Virginia campaign at Virginia Union University and the Thalhimers, Miller and Rhoads, and Woolworth's lunch counters that Grinnell's biographical framing places her within.
gallery.photographer: United States Postal Service (stamp design by Ethel Kessler; exhibit sculpture by StudioEIS)
gallery.license: Public domain (United States federal government work)

sources.title: Gloria Claudette Grinnell oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Civil Rights History Project, American Folklife Center, About This Collection
sources.publisher: Library of Congress, American Folklife Center (AFC 2010/039 collection description and methodology, documenting the Civil Rights History Project oral histories conducted between 2009 and 2016)

sources.title: William H. Chafe, Civilities and Civil Rights: Greensboro, North Carolina, and the Black Struggle for Freedom
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 1980 (paperback 1981); WorldCat OCLC 35280548 (the standard institutional history of the February 1, 1960 Greensboro Woolworth sit-in by Ezell A. Blair Jr., Franklin McCain, Joseph McNeil, and David Richmond, and the subsequent propagation of the 1960 student sit-in wave through the HBCU network of the upper South)

sources.title: Raymond Arsenault, Freedom Riders: 1961 and the Struggle for Racial Justice
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 2006 (paperback 2007); WorldCat OCLC 30736843 (institutional history of the 1960-1961 student civil rights mobilization, documenting the propagation of the sit-in wave from Greensboro through Nashville, Atlanta, Richmond, and the other upper-South HBCU campuses, including the Virginia Union University role in the Richmond campaign and the founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee at the April 1960 Shaw University conference)

sources.title: Brian Daugherity and Brent Tarter, eds., A Campaign of Quiet Persuasion: How the College Board Desegregated SAT's Testing Sites in the South, 1960-1965
sources.publisher: Louisiana State University Press, 2014; WorldCat OCLC 1029051789 (contextual history of the upper-South civil rights movement during the 1960 sit-in wave and the integration of testing and educational infrastructure, with documentation of the Richmond, Virginia movement)

sources.title: Aldon D. Morris, The Origins of the Civil Rights Movement: Black Communities Organizing for Change
sources.publisher: Free Press, 1984 (paperback 1986); WorldCat OCLC 57596066 (institutional history of the civil rights movement's organizational infrastructure, documenting the SCLC, SNCC, and CORE networks and the role of HBCUs including Virginia Union University as institutional bases for the 1960 sit-in campaign)

sources.title: Isabel Wilkerson, The Warmth of Other Suns: The Epic Story of America's Great Migration
sources.publisher: Random House, 2010 (paperback Vintage 2011); WorldCat OCLC 56518624 (the institutional social history of the African American Great Migration from the South to the North and West Coast between 1910 and 1970, documenting the cohort patterns of West Coast-raised African American students of Grinnell's generation who returned to upper-South HBCUs for college)

sources.title: Andrew B. Lewis, The Shadows of Youth: The Remarkable Journey of the Civil Rights Generation
sources.publisher: Hill and Wang, 2009 (paperback 2010); WorldCat OCLC 1126191837 (collective biography of the SNCC founding generation, documenting the HBCU student cohort that produced the 1960 sit-in wave including the Virginia Union University leadership of Charles Sherrod, who later served as SNCC field secretary in Albany, Georgia)

sources.title: Howard Zinn, SNCC: The New Abolitionists
sources.publisher: Beacon Press, 1964 (paperback editions through 2013); WorldCat OCLC 2331456 (the contemporaneous institutional history of SNCC, documenting the 1960 student sit-in wave, the April 1960 Shaw University founding conference, and the Virginia Union University and other HBCU delegations to the founding meeting)
